annotations.label: Power series

anchor.snippet: is an infinite series of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib models analytic power series as FormalMultilinearSeries with HasFPowerSeriesOnBall (and formal ones as PowerSeries), generalizing the scalar series Σ aₙ(x−c)ⁿ.

anchors.snippet: is an infinite series of the form

annotations.label: Borel's theorem

anchor.snippet: Borel's theorem implies that every power series is the Taylor series of some smooth function

annotations.note: Borel's theorem (every formal Taylor series is realized by a smooth function) is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Borel's theorem implies that every power series is the Taylor series of some smooth function

annotations.label: Maclaurin series (center zero)

anchor.snippet: the center c is equal to zero, for instance for Maclaurin series

annotations.note: A Maclaurin series is just a power-series expansion at center 0, the special case x = 0 of HasFPowerSeriesAt, with no dedicated Maclaurin notion.

anchors.snippet: the center c is equal to zero, for instance for Maclaurin series

annotations.label: Polynomial as power series

anchor.snippet: Every polynomial of degree d can be expressed as a power series around any center c

annotations.note: Polynomial.taylor r f re-expresses a polynomial in powers of (X − r) about any center r, with Polynomial.taylor_coeff giving its coefficients.

anchors.snippet: Every polynomial of degree d can be expressed as a power series around any center c

annotations.label: Polynomial expanded about two centers

anchor.snippet: For instance, the polynomial

annotations.note: The specific numeric example is not in Mathlib, but Polynomial.taylor provides the re-centering computation it illustrates.

anchors.snippet: For instance, the polynomial

annotations.label: No negative powers

anchor.snippet: Negative powers are not permitted in an ordinary power series

annotations.note: PowerSeries is indexed by ℕ so negative exponents are excluded by construction (negative powers live in Mathlib's LaurentSeries instead), but there is no explicit statement.

annotations.label: No fractional powers

anchor.snippet: Similarly, fractional powers such as

annotations.note: PowerSeries / FormalMultilinearSeries are indexed by ℕ, so fractional exponents are excluded by construction with no explicit statement.

annotations.label: Coefficients independent of x

anchor.snippet: must not depend on

annotations.note: Coefficients are elements of the base ring (PowerSeries.coeff : R) and hence constant by construction, but this is definitional rather than a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Convergence at center

anchor.snippet: is convergent for some values of x , which always include x = c

annotations.note: Convergence at the center is the trivial value p.sum 0 = p₀ inside the radius-0 ball; no lemma states it specifically.

annotations.label: Existence of radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: there is always a number r with 0 < r ≤ ∞

annotations.note: Every formal series has a well-defined radius p.radius : ℝ≥0∞, so the radius always exists.

anchors.snippet: there is always a number r with 0 < r ≤ ∞

annotations.label: Radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: is called the radius of convergence of the power series

annotations.note: FormalMultilinearSeries.radius is the largest r with ‖pₙ‖rⁿ subexponential, i.e. the radius of convergence.

anchors.snippet: is called the radius of convergence of the power series

annotations.label: Cauchy–Hadamard theorem

anchor.snippet: This is the Cauchy–Hadamard theorem

annotations.note: radius_inv_eq_limsup (and radius_eq_liminf) express 1/r = limsup ‖pₙ‖^(1/n), explicitly labelled the Cauchy–Hadamard theorem.

anchors.snippet: This is the Cauchy–Hadamard theorem

annotations.label: Ratio formula for radius

anchor.snippet: is also satisfied, if this limit exists

annotations.note: For scalar series, ofScalars_radius_eq_inv_of_tendsto gives radius = (lim ‖cₙ₊₁‖/‖cₙ‖)⁻¹ when the limit exists.

anchors.snippet: is also satisfied, if this limit exists

annotations.label: Disc of convergence

anchor.snippet: is called the disc of convergence of the series

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the open ball Metric.eball 0 p.radius (e.g. in FormalMultilinearSeries.analyticOnNhd) but has no separately named 'disc of convergence' definition.

annotations.label: Absolute and uniform convergence

anchor.snippet: The series converges absolutely inside its disc of convergence

annotations.note: summable_norm_apply gives absolute convergence inside the radius and HasFPowerSeriesOnBall.tendstoLocallyUniformlyOn gives locally uniform convergence.

annotations.label: Abel's theorem

anchor.snippet: Abel's theorem states that if the series is convergent

annotations.note: Abel's limit theorem is formalized as Real.tendsto_tsum_powerSeries_nhdsWithin_lt and Complex.tendsto_tsum_powerSeries_nhdsWithin_stolzCone.

annotations.label: Termwise addition and subtraction

anchor.snippet: the power series of the sum or difference of the functions can be obtained by termwise addition and subtraction

annotations.note: HasFPowerSeriesOnBall.add and .sub show that pf + pg / pf − pg is a power series for f ± g.

anchors.snippet: the power series of the sum or difference of the functions can be obtained by termwise addition and subtraction

annotations.label: Radius of convergence of a sum

anchor.snippet: The sum of two power series will have a radius of convergence of at least the smaller of the two radii of convergence

annotations.note: min_radius_le_radius_add states min(p.radius, q.radius) ≤ (p+q).radius.

annotations.label: Product and quotient of power series

anchor.snippet: the power series of the product and quotient of the functions can be obtained as follows

annotations.note: AnalyticAt.mul and AnalyticAt.div show products and quotients of (power-series) analytic functions are again analytic.

anchors.snippet: the power series of the product and quotient of the functions can be obtained as follows

annotations.label: Cauchy product

anchor.snippet: is known as the Cauchy product of the sequences

annotations.note: The Cauchy product formula is tsum_mul_tsum_eq_tsum_sum_antidiagonal, matching the antidiagonal-convolution PowerSeries.coeff_mul.

annotations.label: Division by recursion

anchor.snippet: and one can solve recursively for the terms

annotations.note: PowerSeries.inv / invOfUnit define the reciprocal series whose coefficients are computed recursively (inv.aux).

anchors.snippet: and one can solve recursively for the terms

annotations.label: Determinant formula for division

anchor.snippet: yields the formulae based on determinants of certain matrices of the coefficients

annotations.note: The Hankel/Toeplitz-determinant closed form for power-series division coefficients is not in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: yields the formulae based on determinants of certain matrices of the coefficients

annotations.label: Termwise differentiation and integration

anchor.snippet: It can be differentiated and integrated by treating every term separately

annotations.note: HasFPowerSeriesOnBall.fderiv shows the derivative has the termwise derivative series p.derivSeries as its power series; termwise integration is not separately stated.

anchors.snippet: It can be differentiated and integrated by treating every term separately

annotations.label: Derivative/integral preserve radius

anchor.snippet: Both of these series have the same radius of convergence as the original series

annotations.note: radius_le_radius_derivSeries gives p.radius ≤ p.derivSeries.radius (one direction); the full equality of radii is not stated as a single lemma.

annotations.label: Analytic function

anchor.snippet: is called analytic if it is locally given by a convergent power series

annotations.note: AnalyticAt / AnalyticOnNhd define functions admitting a convergent power-series expansion at / near a point.

annotations.label: Power series sums are analytic

anchor.snippet: Every power series with a positive radius of convergence is analytic on the interior of its region of convergence

annotations.note: FormalMultilinearSeries.analyticOnNhd shows p.sum is analytic on the ball Metric.eball 0 p.radius.

annotations.label: Holomorphic implies complex-analytic

anchor.snippet: All holomorphic functions are complex-analytic

annotations.note: DifferentiableOn.analyticAt / Differentiable.analyticAt prove complex differentiability implies analyticity.

annotations.label: Closure of analytic functions under operations

anchor.snippet: Sums and products of analytic functions are analytic

annotations.note: AnalyticAt.add and AnalyticAt.mul (with .div) show sums and products of analytic functions are analytic.

annotations.label: Analytic implies infinitely differentiable

anchor.snippet: If a function is analytic, then it is infinitely differentiable

annotations.note: AnalyticAt.contDiffAt / AnalyticOnNhd.contDiffOn give C^∞ smoothness of analytic functions.

anchors.snippet: If a function is analytic, then it is infinitely differentiable

annotations.label: Taylor coefficient formula

anchor.snippet: For an analytic function, the coefficients a n can be computed as

annotations.note: factorial_smul gives n! • pₙ = iteratedFDeriv n f x, i.e. aₙ = f⁽ⁿ⁾(c)/n!.

anchors.snippet: For an analytic function, the coefficients a n can be computed as

annotations.label: Local Taylor representation

anchor.snippet: every analytic function is locally represented by its Taylor series

annotations.note: hasSum_iteratedFDeriv shows f(x+y) = Σ (1/n!) iteratedFDeriv n f x (y,…,y), the local Taylor expansion.

annotations.label: Identity theorem for analytic functions

anchor.snippet: The global form of an analytic function is completely determined by its local behavior

annotations.note: The identity principle is AnalyticOnNhd.eqOn_of_preconnected_of_eventuallyEq (and the eqOn_zero variants).

annotations.label: Maximality of the radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: The number r is maximal in the following sense

annotations.note: There is no Mathlib theorem asserting the existence of a singularity on the boundary circle / non-extendability beyond the radius.

annotations.label: Lagrange inversion theorem

anchor.snippet: can be determined using the Lagrange inversion theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs the inverse series (leftInv/rightInv) and proves its convergence, but the explicit Lagrange inversion coefficient formula is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sum analytic in interior

anchor.snippet: The sum of a power series with a positive radius of convergence is an analytic function at every point in the interior

annotations.note: FormalMultilinearSeries.analyticOnNhd states p.sum is analytic at every point of the open disc of convergence.

annotations.label: Divergent but extends analytically

anchor.snippet: Divergence while the sum extends to an analytic function

annotations.note: This specific boundary-behavior example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convergent at some boundary points

anchor.snippet: Convergent at some points divergent at others

annotations.note: This specific boundary-behavior example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Absolute convergence on boundary

anchor.snippet: Absolute convergence at every point of the boundary

annotations.note: This specific boundary-behavior example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sierpiński's example

anchor.snippet: Sierpiński gave an example

annotations.note: Sierpiński's boundary example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abel's theorem (boundary continuity)

anchor.snippet: A sufficient condition for one-sided continuity at a boundary point is given by Abel's theorem

annotations.note: Abel's limit theorem giving one-sided boundary continuity is Real.tendsto_tsum_powerSeries_nhdsWithin_lt / Complex stolzCone version.

annotations.label: Generating series

anchor.snippet: It is common to associate to any sequence of numbers

annotations.note: PowerSeries.mk associates to any sequence f : ℕ → R its (ordinary) generating power series.

anchors.snippet: It is common to associate to any sequence of numbers

annotations.label: Exponential generating series

anchor.snippet: or its exponential generating series

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated exponential-generating-function construction (only ad hoc rescale/PowerSeries.exp usages).

anchors.snippet: or its exponential generating series

annotations.label: Positive radius gives analytic function

anchor.snippet: When the radius of convergence is positive, the series defines an analytic function

annotations.note: For positive radius, FormalMultilinearSeries.hasFPowerSeriesOnBall (and analyticOnNhd) show p.sum is analytic on the disc.

annotations.label: Formal power series

anchor.snippet: This leads to the concept of formal power series

annotations.note: PowerSeries R is the ring of formal power series over R.

annotations.label: Power series in several variables

anchor.snippet: A power series is here defined to be an infinite series of the form

annotations.note: MvPowerSeries σ R is the multivariate (formal) power series ring indexed by multi-indices; analytic several-variable series are covered by FormalMultilinearSeries.

anchors.snippet: A power series is here defined to be an infinite series of the form

annotations.label: Convergence between two hyperbolas

anchor.snippet: is absolutely convergent in the set

annotations.note: This specific two-variable convergence-region example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Region of convergence is log-convex

anchor.snippet: the interior of the region of absolute convergence is always a log-convex set

annotations.note: Mathlib explicitly does not define the multivariate polydisk/region of convergence, so its log-convexity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Term-by-term calculus in several variables

anchor.snippet: one may differentiate and integrate under the series sign

annotations.note: The FormalMultilinearSeries framework allows term-by-term differentiation in arbitrary (multivariate) normed spaces via derivSeries, but there is no statement specific to multivariate scalar power series.

annotations.label: Order of a power series

anchor.snippet: The order of the power series f is defined to be the least value

annotations.note: PowerSeries.order is the least n with nonzero coefficient (greatest n with Xⁿ ∣ f), ⊤ for the zero series.
